elements.text:
Introduction to Retail 101 - Core Process Flows

Hello, and welcome to this video-led learning program.
This program - Retail 101 - is an essential foundation for anyone working in the retail domain.
We'll be covering five core process flows that are key to understanding how retail works:

elements.process_name: Return Management Process Flow
elements.text: Throughout the program, you'll often hear two important terms - Key Actors and Systems:

elements.process_name: Return Management Process Flow
elements.text: Key Actors are the roles where human interaction is involved in the retail process.

elements.process_name: Return Management Process Flow
elements.text:
Systems refer to the workflows and automated processes that keep retail operations running smoothly.

In every process flow, blue boxes will represent the Actors involved, and orange boxes will represent the Systems enabling those activities.

elements.process_name: Slide 1: Introduction to Inventory Management - The Inventory Management Process Flow is about keeping track of products. Retail 101 Training

elements.slide_title: An Introduction to the Retail Sector
elements.text: Slide 1 - An Introduction to the Retail Sector
Slide Content (shown on PPT):
Retail Ecosystem & Value Chain - from manufacturers to customers
Retail Formats - supermarkets, convenience stores, e-commerce, omnichannel
Retail Processes - procurement, inventory, fulfillment, customer service
Retail Systems - POS, ERP, OMS & supporting technologies
Regulations & KPIs - compliance & performance measures
Emerging Trends - digital tech, data-driven practices, evolving expectations

Narration Script (to be spoken):
"Welcome to this Retail 101 program. This session will give you a solid foundation in how the retail sector works.
We'll start by understanding the retail ecosystem and value chain - how goods and value flow from manufacturers all the way to customers.
Next, we'll look at different retail formats, including supermarkets, convenience stores, e-commerce platforms, and omnichannel models that combine online and offline experiences.
We'll then dive into the core retail processes like procurement, inventory management, order fulfillment, and customer service - all of which ensure smooth operations.
Supporting these processes are critical retail systems such as POS, ERP, and OMS that help retailers run efficiently.
We'll also touch upon regulations and KPIs - covering compliance requirements and how performance is measured.
Finally, we'll explore emerging trends, such as the use of digital technologies, data-driven practices, and how retailers are adapting to changing customer expectations.
This introduction sets the stage for the deeper dive into each area in the upcoming slides."

Slide 2 - Agenda - Retail 101 Training
Slide Content (shown on PPT):
Module 1: Introduction to Retail
Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies
Module 3: Core Retail Concepts
Module 4: Retail Operations
Module 5: Governance & Performance
Module 6: Retail Landscape & Future

Module 7: Wrap-up & Knowledge Check

Narration

"Here's the agenda for our Retail 101 training.
We'll begin with Module 1, where we introduce the retail ecosystem, its importance, the evolution of retail, and the different retail formats.
Module 2 will familiarize you with the most common retail terminologies and acronyms like POS, SKU, and ERP, helping you understand how they're used in daily operations.
In Module 3, we'll dive into core retail concepts, including the retail value chain, customer journey, and assortment management.
Module 4 is about retail operations - we'll cover supply chain basics, key retail systems like POS, OMS, WMS, ERP, and CRM, and also look at omnichannel retail, payments, private labels, and loss prevention.
Module 5 focuses on governance and performance, where we'll discuss compliance requirements and the KPIs used to measure retail success.
Module 6 will take a broader view of the retail landscape - looking at global and Indian leaders, the role of technology, and emerging trends shaping the future of retail.
Finally, in Module 7, we'll wrap up the program with a summary of key learnings, and conduct a short knowledge check or Q&A to reinforce your understanding."

Slide 3 - PPT Content (on slide)

Welcome to Module 1: Introduction to Retail
This module covers:
Retail Ecosystem - Overview
Importance of Retail
Evolution of Retail

Types of Retail Formats

Narration

"Welcome to Module 1: Introduction to Retail.
This module is designed to give you the foundation you need before we move into detailed retail processes.
We'll be covering four key areas: the retail ecosystem, the importance of retail, its evolution over time, and different retail formats.
Before we dive deeper, let's start with the Retail Ecosystem. Think of it as the big picture -- all the players and functions working together to deliver a product or service to the end consumer.
In the next slide, we'll explore how this ecosystem flows step by step, from the manufacturer all the way to the customer."
Slide 4 - PPT Content (on slide)
Retail Ecosystem: Overview
Retail = selling goods/services directly to consumers (final stage of supply chain).
Flow: Manufacturer → Wholesaler/Distributor → Retailer → Customer.
Key Enablers: Logistics | Finance | IT (POS/ERP) | Marketing.
Retail is customer-facing - trust, convenience, and brand perception depend on it.
Example: Nike - manufactured in Vietnam → shipped to warehouses → sold via Nike stores/online → customer purchase defines the retail moment.
Narration (to be spoken)
"Retail is the process of selling goods and services directly to end consumers. It represents the final stage of the supply chain -- where products finally reach the people who use them.
The flow is simple: manufacturers create products, which then move to wholesalers or distributors, then to retailers, and finally to customers.
Supporting this flow are key enablers such as logistics for storage and transport, finance for payments and credit, IT systems like POS and ERP for smooth operations, and marketing to drive demand.

What makes retail unique is that it is customer-facing. Retailers are not just moving goods; they are building trust and convenience at the last mile. A poor retail experience can damage the perception of the entire brand.

Take Nike, for example. Shoes may be manufactured in Vietnam, shipped to Nike warehouses, and then sold through Nike's own stores or online platforms. The customer's final buying experience -- whether in-store or online -- is what defines retail.

In essence, retail is the bridge between the world of production and the world of consumption."

Slide 5 - Importance of Retail
Key Points:
Economic Growth - Major GDP contributor (India: ~10%, US: ~20%).
Employment - 2nd largest employer in India after agriculture.
Customer Value - Offers choice, convenience, and competitive pricing.
Innovation Hub - Drives change (e.g., Amazon Go, Zepto's 10-min delivery).

Narration 
"Retail is much more than just selling products. It's a powerful engine of the economy, contributing around 10% of India's GDP and nearly 20% in the US. It also creates millions of jobs, making it the second-largest employer in India after agriculture. For customers, retail ensures choice, convenience, and competitive pricing across multiple formats - whether offline, online, or through quick commerce. And beyond that, retail is constantly innovating. New models like Amazon Go's cashier-less stores or Zepto's 10-minute delivery are redefining consumer expectations every day."

Slide 6 - Evolution of Retail (1/3): Traditional Retail
PPT Content:
Dominated by kirana & mom-and-pop stores.
Built on trust, relationships, and community.
Customers often purchased on credit.
Personalized shopping experience.
Focus on convenience & reliability, not discounts.
Example: Local kirana delivering groceries & maintaining handwritten dues.

Narration:
"In the early stages, retail in India and across the world was dominated by small kirana shops and mom-and-pop stores. These stores were deeply community-driven, built on trust and personal relationships. Customers often purchased items on credit, with payments settled later. The experience was highly personalized, as shopkeepers usually knew the family's preferences. The focus was not variety or discounts, but rather convenience and reliability. A good example is your neighborhood kirana store delivering groceries at home and keeping a handwritten record of dues."
Slide 7 - Evolution of Retail (2/3): Organized Retail
PPT Content:
Emerged with urbanization & economic growth.
Department Stores (1800s): Multiple categories, destination shopping.
Supermarkets/Hypermarkets (1900s): Self-service, bulk buying, discount pricing.
Discount Stores: Low prices, high volumes, value-conscious customers.
Examples: Walmart (global), Big Bazaar & DMart (India).

Narration:
"With urbanization and economic development, retail formats became more structured and organized. Department stores in the 1800s offered multiple categories under one roof, making them a one-stop destination. In the 1900s, supermarkets and hypermarkets introduced self-service, bulk buying, and discount pricing, turning shopping into an experience. Discount stores also emerged, focusing on affordability and attracting value-conscious shoppers. For example, Walmart became a global leader in supply chain efficiency, while in India, Big Bazaar popularized hypermarkets and DMart built its success on everyday low pricing."

elements.slide_title: Evolution of Retail (3/3): Digital & Future Retail
elements.text: Slide 8 - Evolution of Retail (3/3): Digital & Future Retail
PPT Content:
Technology shifted retail to digital-first.
E-commerce (2000s): Amazon, Flipkart, Myntra - shopping anytime, anywhere.
Omnichannel (2010s): Integrated online & offline journey.
Future Retail: AI personalization, AR/VR shopping, robotics, data-driven insights.
Example: AR try-on features, AI chatbots, Amazon robotics.

Narration:
"The next big transformation came with technology, moving retail from physical-first to digital-first. In the 2000s, e-commerce platforms like Amazon, Flipkart, and Myntra allowed customers to shop anytime, anywhere, breaking geographical barriers. The 2010s brought omnichannel retail, integrating offline and online journeys -- for example, browsing online but purchasing in-store or choosing click-and-collect options. Looking ahead, future retail will be shaped by AI-driven personalization, AR and VR shopping experiences, robotics in warehouses, and advanced data analytics. Examples include AR try-on features in fashion apps, AI chatbots for personalized recommendations, and Amazon's robotic warehouses."
Slide 9 - Types of Retail Formats (1/2)
PPT Content:
Department Stores: Multiple categories under one roof.
Supermarkets: Groceries & essentials, self-service, competitive pricing.
Hypermarkets: Larger scale, groceries + clothing + electronics, bulk sales & discounts.
Examples: Shoppers Stop (India), Walmart (US), Carrefour (global), Big Bazaar & DMart (India).

Narration:
"Now let's look at the different types of retail formats. Department stores offer multiple categories under one roof -- from clothing and cosmetics to footwear and home furnishings. A good example is Shoppers Stop in India. Supermarkets focus mainly on groceries and daily essentials, usually in a self-service format with attractive pricing -- think Walmart in the US or Big Bazaar in India. Hypermarkets take this a step further, operating on a much larger scale. They combine groceries, clothing, electronics, and even appliances, with an emphasis on bulk sales and big discounts. Examples include Carrefour globally and DMart in India."
Slide 10 - Types of Retail Formats (2/2)
Content (for PPT):
Discount Stores → Low prices, high volumes (e.g., Dollar Tree, DMart)
Specialty Stores → Focus on single category, expertise & variety (e.g., Nike, Sephora)
Convenience Stores → Small format, quick buys near homes/offices (e.g., 7-Eleven)
E-commerce → Online shopping, price comparison, home delivery (e.g., Amazon, Flipkart)
Omnichannel → Seamless online + offline journey (e.g., Target, Reliance Digital)

Narration (for delivery):
"Moving forward, let's explore more formats.

We begin with Discount Stores, which are built around the idea of low prices and high volumes, making them highly attractive to price-sensitive customers. Think of Dollar Tree in the US or DMart in India.

Next are Specialty Stores. These focus on a single product category and build deep expertise, offering wide variety and enhanced customer experiences. Examples include Nike for sportswear or Sephora for beauty.
Then we have Convenience Stores. These are small outlets designed for quick purchases, often strategically located near homes or workplaces. 7-Eleven is a global leader in this space.
Of course, E-commerce has revolutionized retail. Online platforms like Amazon and Flipkart make it easy to shop from anywhere, compare prices, and get doorstep delivery.

And finally, we have Omnichannel Retail, which is the latest trend. It combines offline and online seamlessly - so customers can check availability online, try in-store, and even get home delivery. Retailers like Target in the US and Reliance Digital in India are actively adopting this model."

Slide 11 - 
Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies
Content (for PPT):
Retail has its own vocabulary
Used by managers, buyers, supply chain, and tech teams
Learn common acronyms & terms: POS, SKU, ERP, CRM, etc.

Narration (for delivery):
"Welcome to Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies. Just like every profession has its own language, retail too has a vocabulary that's widely used by store managers, buyers, supply chain teams, and even technology partners. In this module, we'll get familiar with the most important retail terms and acronyms--like POS, SKU, ERP, CRM, and many more--that you'll come across in day-to-day retail operations."
Slide 12 - Store & Product Related Terms
Content (for PPT):
SKU (Stock Keeping Unit): Unique ID per product variant
UPC / Barcode: Standardized code for scanning & billing
Category Management: Grouping products to improve sales
Planogram (POG): Guide for store product placement
Private Label: Retailer-owned brands
Assortment: Range of products offered
Facing: Number of product units visible on shelf

Narration (for delivery):
"Let's begin with some store and product-related terms.
First, SKU or Stock Keeping Unit - this is a unique ID for each product variant, like a blue shirt in size M.
Next, UPC or Barcode - this is the standardized code you see on products, used for scanning and billing at the checkout.
Then, Category Management - the practice of logically grouping products to optimize sales and make shopping easier for customers.
We also have the Planogram, or POG, which is essentially a store layout guide showing exactly where products should be placed on shelves.
Another key concept is Private Label, which refers to retailer-owned brands--for example, Amazon Basics or DMart Ready.
Assortment refers to the range of products that a store or online platform offers.
And finally, Facing is the number of product units visible to customers on the shelf--it's about presentation and visibility."
Slide 13 - Sales & Checkout Terms
Content (for PPT) →
POS (Point of Sale): Billing system (cash registers, digital POS).
Basket Size: Average number of items per transaction.
Footfall: Number of customers visiting a store.
Conversion Rate: % of visitors who actually purchase.
Shrinkage: Inventory loss due to theft, damage, or errors.
AOV (Average Order Value): Average spend per customer order.
Cross-selling / Upselling: Suggesting additional or premium products at checkout.

Narration (for delivery) 

Now, let's move into some sales and checkout terms that retailers use every day:
POS, or Point of Sale, refers to the system used for billing--this could be a traditional cash register, or a digital POS that supports card payments, UPI, or even mobile wallets.
Basket Size shows how many items, on average, a customer buys in a single transaction. A higher basket size usually means better sales per visit.
Footfall is simply the count of people walking into a store. But what really matters is how many of them actually buy something--this is tracked by the Conversion Rate.
Shrinkage is a challenge for retailers--it means loss of inventory because of theft, damage, or clerical errors.
AOV, or Average Order Value, tracks the average amount spent per transaction. For example, if a store has 100 orders worth $5,000 in total, the AOV is $50.

Finally, cross-selling and upselling are sales techniques at checkout. Cross-selling is about recommending a related product--like offering fries with a burger--while upselling is about suggesting a premium option, like a bigger size or higher-end model.

Slide 14 - Supply Chain & Inventory Terms
Content (for PPT):
ERP: Integrated system for operations.
RFQ: Requesting supplier price quotes.
PO: Official order sent to supplier.
GRN: Record of goods received.
WMS: Manages warehouse storage & movement.
OMS: Manages customer orders across channels.
FIFO: Oldest stock sold first.
Lead Time: Time from order to receipt.

Narration (for delivery):
"In retail, supply chain and inventory terms are crucial for smooth operations.
ERP, or Enterprise Resource Planning, is the integrated backbone system that connects procurement, finance, inventory, and sales.
RFQ is a request for suppliers to share their price quotes, while a PO or Purchase Order is the formal document that confirms the purchase.
When goods arrive, they are documented through a GRN, or Goods Receipt Note.
Inside the warehouse, a WMS, or Warehouse Management System, keeps track of how items are stored, moved, and dispatched.
The OMS, or Order Management System, handles customer orders across online and offline channels.
FIFO, First In First Out, ensures older stock is sold before new stock, reducing waste.
And finally, Lead Time measures the time taken between placing an order and actually receiving it -- a key factor for planning stock levels."
Slide 15 - Marketing & Customer Terms
Content (for PPT):
CRM: Tracks customer interactions.
Loyalty Programs: Rewards for retention.
Merchandising: Displays, offers, pricing.
Promotion Calendar: Scheduled discounts & campaigns.
CLV: Total long-term customer value.
Market Basket Analysis: Products often bought together.
Churn Rate: % of customers lost.

Narration (for delivery):
"Retail success heavily depends on customer-focused strategies.
CRM, or Customer Relationship Management, helps retailers track interactions and history with each customer.
Loyalty programs such as reward points or cashback encourage customers to keep coming back.
Merchandising focuses on how products are displayed, priced, and promoted inside the store.
A promotion calendar is a planned schedule of discounts, festive offers, and campaigns.
Customer Lifetime Value, or CLV, estimates how much revenue a single customer contributes over their entire relationship with the retailer.
Market Basket Analysis helps identify products that are frequently purchased together -- for example, chips and soft drinks.
And lastly, Churn Rate shows the percentage of customers who stop buying, helping retailers know when they need to improve retention efforts."
Slide 16 - Omnichannel & Digital Terms
Content (for PPT):
Omnichannel: Seamless online + offline shopping.
Click & Collect / BOPIS: Order online, pick up in-store.
Dropshipping: Supplier ships directly to customer.
Last Mile Delivery: Final delivery to customer's doorstep.
Endless Aisle: Order out-of-stock items via kiosk/app.
Dark Store: Store used only for fulfilling online orders.

Narration (for delivery):
"Today's retail is driven by digital and omnichannel strategies.
Omnichannel retailing means giving customers a seamless shopping experience across both online and offline platforms.
Click and Collect, or BOPIS, allows customers to order online and pick up items at a nearby store.
Dropshipping is a model where the retailer sells products, but the supplier ships them directly to the customer.
Last Mile Delivery refers to the critical final step of getting the product to the customer's doorstep.
Endless Aisle enables customers to order items that aren't physically available in-store, usually via kiosks or mobile apps.
And a Dark Store is essentially a retail space dedicated only to fulfilling online orders, with no walk-in customers.
These digital innovations are shaping the future of retail."

elements.slide_title: Module 3 - Introduction to Retail Value Chain
elements.text:
Slide 17
Module 3 - Introduction to Retail Value Chain
Content (for PPT)
Covers the complete journey: Supplier → Customer.
Includes product, money, and information flow.
Key Stages:
Procurement
Inbound Logistics
Inventory Management
Merchandising
Sales
Fulfillment
Customer Service

Narration (for delivery)
"Welcome to Module 3: Retail Value Chain. In this module, we'll explore the full journey of retail - how a product moves from the supplier to the customer, and how money and information flow alongside. The value chain is made up of seven key stages: Procurement, Inbound Logistics, Inventory Management, Merchandising, Sales, Fulfillment, and finally, Customer Service. Understanding this chain helps us see the bigger picture of how retail businesses operate end-to-end."

Slide 18 - Procurement in Retail
Content (for PPT):
Backbone of retail: sourcing & purchasing products
Step 1: Requirement Identification

Narration (for delivery):
Procurement is the backbone of retail because it decides what products reach the customer and when. Let's break this into steps. First, retailers identify requirements - what product is needed, how much, and by when. For example, a store may need 10,000 shirts before the festive season.

Next, they raise an RFQ, or Request for Quotation, to compare prices, delivery timelines, and quality from different suppliers. Based on this, the retailer selects the supplier offering the best value - balancing cost, quality, and reliability.

Once chosen, a Purchase Order, or PO, is issued. This is a legal contract confirming details like product, price, and delivery dates. When goods arrive, a Goods Receipt Note (GRN) is prepared after checking quantity and quality. Finally, the invoice is verified, and payment is processed.
In short, procurement ensures retailers get the right product, at the right price, at the right time - a foundation for smooth inventory management and customer satisfaction.

Slide 28 - Retail Supply Chain Basics
Content (for PPT) →
Supply chain = backbone of retail → ensures flow of products
Core Flow: Supplier → DC → Warehouse/Store → Customer
Drop Shipping: Direct supplier → customer; low cost, slower delivery

Narration (for delivery) →

The retail supply chain is the backbone of operations. It ensures products move efficiently from suppliers to customers, balancing speed, cost, and availability.

First, the core flow: goods usually travel from suppliers, to distribution centers, then to warehouses or stores, and finally to the customer. This structure balances bulk handling with local availability.
Next, drop shipping - here, the supplier ships directly to the customer. It saves inventory costs for the retailer, though it may result in longer delivery timelines.
Another model is cross-docking, where goods skip warehousing altogether and move straight from inbound trucks to outbound trucks. This reduces storage costs and speeds up delivery.

Finally, the most expensive and customer-visible part - last-mile delivery. This is where products move from a store or hub to the customer's doorstep. Retailers invest heavily here, because delivery speed and accuracy directly shape customer satisfaction.
For instance, Flipkart uses warehouses for fast-moving goods but also drop ships low-demand SKUs, while BigBasket focuses on last-mile efficiency with its own delivery fleet.

Slide 33 - Loss Prevention & Shrinkage in Retail
Content (for PPT) →
Shrinkage = gap between recorded vs actual inventory
Causes:
Shoplifting/theft (customers, employees)
Fraud (fake returns, billing manipulation)
Damage/spoilage (esp. food retail)
Admin errors (wrong entry, miscounts)
Avg shrinkage = 1.5-2% of sales → major loss

CCTV & security staff
RFID & barcode tracking
Regular audits & inventory checks
AI & analytics → detect unusual behavior
Example: Walmart uses AI cameras at self-checkout to reduce theft

Narration (for delivery) →
Shrinkage in retail refers to the difference between what's recorded in inventory and what's actually available. This is a critical issue because retail already works on thin margins, and shrinkage averages 1.5-2% of total sales worldwide.

Shrinkage happens due to multiple reasons - shoplifting or theft, both by customers and sometimes employees; fraudulent activities like fake returns or billing manipulation; damage and spoilage, especially in food retail; and simple administrative errors like wrong data entry or miscounts.
To prevent these losses, retailers use several strategies:
CCTV and security staff to deter theft.
RFID and barcode systems to track product movement.
Regular audits and inventory checks to catch discrepancies early.
And now increasingly, AI and analytics to spot unusual patterns like suspicious returns or self-checkout theft.
For example, Walmart uses AI-enabled cameras at self-checkout counters to detect when customers skip scanning items - significantly reducing theft.
In summary, effective loss prevention not only saves costs but also protects profitability.

Narration (for delivery):
Now, let's focus on inventory KPIs, which help retailers manage stock efficiently and meet customer demand.
First, Inventory Turnover Ratio tells us how fast stock is sold and replenished. Higher turnover means products move quickly, while lower turnover could signal overstocking.
Next, Days of Inventory (DOI) measures how long items stay in inventory before being sold. If this number is high, it means stock is sitting too long, which can tie up capital.
Sell-through percentage compares stock sold to stock received in a period. It helps retailers understand demand and plan inventory better.
Stockout Rate shows how often products are unavailable when customers want them. High stockouts can lead to lost sales and unhappy customers.
Backorder Rate measures orders delayed because stock wasn't available. It reflects supply chain efficiency and planning.
GMROI, or Gross Margin Return on Inventory, tells us the profit earned for every rupee or dollar invested in inventory. This helps retailers evaluate the profitability of their stock.
Finally, Shrinkage percentage highlights losses from theft, damage, or errors. Controlling shrinkage is important to protect profits.
By understanding these KPIs, retailers can balance having enough stock to satisfy customers while keeping inventory costs under control.

Cost per Acquisition (CPA) - Cost incurred to acquire one new customer

Narration (for delivery):
Next, we focus on finance KPIs, which measure the financial health and profitability of a retail business.
Gross Margin % shows the profit remaining after deducting the cost of goods sold. It indicates how efficiently products are priced and sourced.
Operating Margin measures profitability after accounting for operating expenses, helping retailers assess overall efficiency.
Return on Investment (ROI) evaluates how effectively capital is being used to generate returns, guiding investment decisions.
Cost per Acquisition (CPA) calculates the expense to acquire a new customer, helping retailers manage marketing spend and profitability.
Tracking these financial KPIs ensures that retail operations remain profitable and sustainable.

Today, modern retail focuses on being customer-first, leveraging technology, and continuously improving performance to stay competitive.
Retail Domain FAQs
A. Retail Fundamentals

elements.question: What is retail, and how is it different from wholesale?
elements.answer: Retail involves selling goods directly to consumers in small quantities, while wholesale refers to bulk selling to other businesses.

elements.question: What are the major retail formats?
elements.answer: Department stores, supermarkets, specialty stores, ecommerce platforms, factory outlets, convenience stores, and pop-ups.

elements.question: What is an omnichannel retail strategy?
elements.answer: An approach that offers customers a unified experience across channels -- store, website, mobile app, and customer service.

elements.question: What is a product lifecycle in retail?
elements.answer: The progression of a product through stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline -- affecting pricing and inventory decisions.

elements.question: What is seasonality in retail, and how does it affect planning?
elements.answer: Certain periods (festivals, weather changes) see sales spikes. Retailers adjust inventory, staffing, and promotions accordingly.

elements.question: What is a customer journey in retail?
elements.answer: It's the full experience a customer has with a brand -- from discovery and purchase to post-sale service and repeat engagement.

elements.text: B. Retail Operations & Order Fulfillment

elements.question: What is the order lifecycle in retail?
elements.answer: Order placement → confirmation → picking → packing → shipping → delivery → possible return/refund.

elements.question: What are BOPIS, ROPIS, and dropshipping?
elements.answer:
BOPIS: Buy Online, Pick Up In Store
ROPIS: Reserve Online, Pick Up In Store
Dropshipping: Supplier ships directly to customer, bypassing retailer's inventory.

elements.question: What is the role of the POS system in a retail store?
elements.answer: Processes billing, inventory updates, discounts, loyalty tracking, and real-time sales capture at the store.

elements.question: What is WMS (Warehouse Management System)?
elements.answer: It manages inventory flow in the warehouse -- including receiving, slotting, picking, packing, and shipping.

elements.question: What is reverse logistics?
elements.answer: The process of returning goods from the customer back to the retailer or supplier -- involving inspection, restocking, or refund.

elements.question: What does the 'last mile' in delivery mean?
elements.answer: The final leg of delivery -- from the distribution hub or store to the customer's doorstep. It's key to customer satisfaction.

elements.text: C. Merchandising, Inventory & Supply Chain

elements.question: What is a SKU and why is it important?
elements.answer: A Stock Keeping Unit is a unique code assigned to each product variant -- vital for inventory tracking and reporting.

elements.question: What is assortment planning?
elements.answer: Strategic selection of products to stock in a store based on location, demand, season, and customer preferences.

elements.question: What is the difference between ATP, on-hand inventory, and safety stock?
elements.answer:
ATP: Available to Promise -- sellable stock after accounting for committed stock
On-Hand: Physical quantity available
Safety Stock: Extra inventory held to prevent stockouts

elements.question: What is inventory turnover, and why does it matter?
elements.answer: It shows how often stock is sold and replenished. A higher rate indicates better inventory efficiency.

elements.question: What is a planogram?
elements.answer: A visual guide for how products should be placed on shelves to maximize visibility and sales consistency across stores.

elements.question: What is lead time in procurement?
elements.answer: The time between placing a purchase order and receiving the goods. Shorter lead times improve responsiveness.

elements.text: D. Retail KPIs & Performance Metrics

elements.question: What are the top retail KPIs to track?
elements.answer: GMROI, Average Order Value (AOV), Conversion Rate, Sales per Sq. Ft., Forecast Accuracy, Churn Rate, and Sell-Through Rate.

elements.question: What is GMROI and why is it important?
elements.answer: Gross Margin Return on Investment indicates how much profit a retailer earns for every ₹1 invested in inventory.

elements.question: What's the difference between sell-in and sell-out?
elements.answer:
Sell-In: Sale from brand to retailer
Sell-Out: Sale from retailer to end customer

elements.question: What is markdown optimization?
elements.answer: Data-driven strategy to reduce prices at the right time -- clearing stock while maintaining margins.

elements.question: How is promotion effectiveness measured?
elements.answer: Using metrics like lift in sales, redemption rates, profit margins, and customer conversion during campaigns.

elements.question: What is forecast accuracy, and why is it critical?
elements.answer: It measures how well projected demand matches actual sales -- ensuring the right inventory at the right time.

elements.text: E. Systems, Roles & Domain Understanding

elements.question: What are the key systems used in retail tech stacks?
elements.answer: POS (Point of Sale), OMS (Order Management), WMS (Warehouse Management), ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning), CRM (Customer Relationship Management).

elements.question: What roles are involved in a retail transformation project?
elements.answer: Business Analysts, Developers, Testers, Product Owners, Visual Merchandisers, Store Operations, and Inventory Planners.

elements.question: Why is QA critical in retail applications?
elements.answer: Even small bugs in pricing, stock display, or checkout can result in revenue loss or customer dissatisfaction.

elements.question: What is the role of a merchandiser in retail?
elements.answer: To decide what products to sell, when, where, and at what price -- ensuring availability and alignment with customer demand.

elements.question: What is the importance of system integration in retail?
elements.answer: Integration ensures data flow across POS, OMS, ERP, CRM -- preventing mismatches in pricing, stock, and orders.

elements.question: Why is domain knowledge important for BAs, QAs, and Devs in retail?
elements.answer: It helps them build smarter, user-centric solutions and test real-world flows accurately (e.g., returns, promotions, inventory updates).

elements.title: Promo Not Applied at POS
elements.scenario_text: A customer buys 3 T-shirts during a "Buy 2 Get 1 Free" offer. The POS system charges for all 3 items.
elements.what_went_wrong: The promotion rule was not updated or synced with the POS system.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Store Associate, POS, Promo Engine, BA, QA, Developer
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: System should apply the offer automatically
 Actual: POS billed full amount
elements.impact: Customer dissatisfaction, refund needed, negative store experience

elements.title: System Crash During Billing
elements.scenario_text: While billing during a weekend rush, the POS system crashes and restarts. The customer waits, and the queue builds up.
elements.what_went_wrong: POS system failed under high load.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Store Associate, POS, IT Support, BA, QA, Developer
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: POS should work smoothly even during peak time
Actual: POS crashed, delaying transactions
elements.impact: Lost sales, long queues, frustrated customers and staff

elements.title: Item Barcode Not Scanning
elements.scenario_text: Cashier tries to scan a product, but the system shows "Item not found."
elements.what_went_wrong: SKU exists in the backend but hasn't synced with the POS system.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Store Associate, POS, Product Master, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Product details should appear with scan
Actual: System didn't recognize the barcode
elements.impact: Billing delay, pricing error risk, customer frustration

elements.title: Manual Discount Entry Error
elements.scenario_text: Store staff manually applies a 10% discount, but by mistake, enters 100%.
elements.what_went_wrong: POS allowed full discount without confirmation or approval.
elements.who_was_involved: Store Associate, POS, Store Manager, BA, Developer
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: System should validate high discount entries
Actual: Wrong discount applied, item given for free
elements.impact: Revenue loss, audit issue, manual override needed

elements.title: Payment Not Reflected in POS
elements.scenario_text: Customer pays via UPI. The amount is deducted from the customer's bank, but POS shows no confirmation.
elements.what_went_wrong: There was a delay or error in the response from the payment gateway to POS.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Store Associate, POS, Payment Gateway, BA, Developer
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: POS should show "Payment Successful" after confirmation
Actual: Payment status stuck in "Pending"
elements.impact: Customer confusion, risk of duplicate payment, queue delay

elements.title: BOPIS Order Not Available at Store
elements.scenario_text: A customer places a Buy Online, Pick Up In Store order for a jacket. When they reach the store, the item is already sold to a walk-in customer.
elements.what_went_wrong: Inventory was not reserved when the online order was placed, leading to double selling.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Store Associate, OMS, POS, Inventory System, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Item should be reserved immediately upon online order
Actual: Item was sold in-store before reservation
elements.impact: Lost sale, frustrated customer, negative trust in omnichannel services

elements.title: Online Checkout Fails on Payment
elements.scenario_text: Customer adds items to cart, proceeds to checkout, selects UPI, and the payment fails. No retry option is shown and the cart gets cleared.
elements.what_went_wrong: No retry logic or backup payment option was enabled. Session timed out.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, eCom Platform, Payment Gateway, BA, QA, Developer
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Option to retry payment or select another method
Actual: Checkout failed and cart was emptied
elements.impact: Cart abandonment, lost revenue, negative experience

elements.title: Website Shows Product In Stock but Order Gets Cancelled
elements.scenario_text: A customer orders a pair of shoes online. Later, they receive a message saying "Order Cancelled - Out of Stock".
elements.what_went_wrong: Product appeared in stock due to delayed inventory updates from the store or warehouse.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, OMS, Website, Inventory System, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Website should show accurate stock status
Actual: Product was sold out, but site showed availability
elements.impact: Customer disappointment, reputation damage, support overhead

elements.title: Wrong Item Delivered for Online Order
elements.scenario_text: Customer orders a blue shirt (size M) online, but receives a green shirt (size L).
elements.what_went_wrong: Wrong item was picked or packed in warehouse. No validation at packing stage.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Picker/Packer, WMS, Courier, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Item packed should match online order
Actual: Incorrect item delivered
elements.impact: Return request raised, delivery cost doubled, customer inconvenience

elements.title: Customer Can't Track Their Order
elements.scenario_text: After placing an order, the customer receives no SMS/email updates and the order tracking link doesn't work.
elements.what_went_wrong: Tracking details were not pushed to the frontend due to API failure or delay in courier integration.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, OMS, Courier Partner, Notification Engine, BA, Dev
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Order tracking should update in real-time
Actual: No visibility on order status
elements.impact: High support calls, negative feedback, anxious customer

elements.title: Order Not Routed to Nearest Store
elements.scenario_text: Customer from Mumbai places an online order. Instead of routing the order to the nearby Mumbai store, OMS routes it to a warehouse in Delhi.
elements.what_went_wrong: Order routing logic didn't prioritize geo-location or nearest inventory.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, OMS, Inventory System, Fulfillment Partner, BA, Dev
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Order should be routed to closest fulfillment location
Actual: Order sent from a faraway warehouse, increasing delivery time
elements.impact: Delayed delivery, higher logistics cost, poor customer experience

elements.title: Customer Unable to Cancel an Order
elements.scenario_text: A customer tries to cancel their order within 30 minutes of placing it, but the site shows "Order already shipped."
elements.what_went_wrong: OMS did not respect the cancellation window and passed the order to fulfillment too early.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, OMS, Website, Warehouse, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Cancellation should be allowed within window
Actual: Order shipped instantly, making cancellation impossible
elements.impact: Customer dissatisfaction, forced returns, waste of shipping cost

elements.title: Duplicate Order Created
elements.scenario_text: Customer clicks "Place Order" once, but due to network lag, the system creates two identical orders and charges twice.
elements.what_went_wrong: OMS didn't block duplicate order creation caused by repeated clicks.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, OMS, Website, Payment Gateway, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: One order and one payment should be created
Actual: Two orders placed, two payments deducted

elements.title: Partial Shipment Not Communicated
elements.scenario_text: Customer ordered 3 items. Only 2 were shipped, but the tracking page still shows "Order shipped" without details.
elements.what_went_wrong: OMS didn't push partial shipment status clearly to the front end.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, OMS, Frontend, Warehouse, Notification Engine
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: System should show partial delivery and pending item(s)
Actual: Customer sees full order marked as shipped
elements.impact: Customer confusion, support queries, poor transparency

elements.title: Refund Not Triggered After Return
elements.scenario_text: Customer returns an item in-store and receives confirmation, but the refund is not credited even after 7 days.
elements.what_went_wrong: Return status updated in POS, but OMS failed to trigger the refund workflow.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Store Associate, POS, OMS, Finance System, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Refund process should start automatically after return
Actual: Refund stuck due to missing event trigger in OMS
elements.impact: Customer frustration, support escalation, risk of brand damage

elements.title: Stock Shows Available but Item is Not Found in Store
elements.scenario_text: Customer places an order for pickup at a store where the system shows the item is in stock. However, when the associate checks, the item is missing.
elements.what_went_wrong: Inventory was not updated after a damaged or misplaced item was removed physically but still reflected in the system.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Store Associate, Inventory System, OMS, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: System should show actual available stock
Actual: Item was marked available but wasn't physically present
elements.impact: Order cancelled, poor customer experience, lost sale

elements.title: Overstocking at One Store, Stockout at Another
elements.scenario_text: A product is overstocked in one store with low demand but out of stock in a nearby high-demand store.
elements.what_went_wrong: Demand forecast was applied uniformly without location-specific sales patterns.
elements.who_was_involved: Inventory Planning Team, Warehouse, Stores, BA, Dev
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Inventory distributed based on demand per location
Actual: Mismatch in supply and demand
elements.impact: Loss of sales, high holding cost, inter-store transfer overhead

elements.title: Automatic Replenishment Didn't Trigger
elements.scenario_text: A fast-selling item reaches below minimum threshold at a store, but no replenishment order is generated.
elements.what_went_wrong: Reorder rule was misconfigured or disabled in the system.
elements.who_was_involved: Inventory System, Store, Supply Chain Team, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Auto-replenishment should trigger once threshold is hit
Actual: No replenishment; stockout occurred
elements.impact: Loss of revenue, customer dissatisfaction, manual intervention required

elements.title: Vendor Delivered Items Without Barcode Tags
elements.scenario_text: A vendor delivers a bulk order, but many items are missing barcodes and SKU tags.
elements.what_went_wrong: Vendor ignored packaging standards; receiving team couldn't scan items into system.
elements.who_was_involved: Vendor, Warehouse Team, Inventory System, Vendor Management Team
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Items delivered with proper SKU labeling
Actual: Manual work needed to tag and upload SKU data
elements.impact: Delay in stocking, chances of SKU mismatch, inventory errors

elements.title: Negative Inventory Value in System
elements.scenario_text: Inventory system shows -2 quantity for a product after multiple returns and cancellations.
elements.what_went_wrong: Inventory updates were not synchronized properly across systems (POS, OMS, Returns Portal).
elements.who_was_involved: Inventory System, OMS, POS, Return Module, BA, QA, Developer
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Inventory count should never go below 0
Actual: System allowed negative stock due to delayed updates
elements.impact: Incorrect availability on frontend, false commitments, system confusion

elements.title: Wrong Item Picked and Shipped
elements.scenario_text: A customer orders a black wallet, but receives a brown belt instead.
elements.what_went_wrong: Picker selected the wrong item from the shelf due to similar packaging and SKU code mismatch.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Warehouse Picker, WMS, Courier Partner
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Correct item picked, verified, and shipped
Actual: Incorrect item sent, leading to customer return
elements.impact: Extra return cost, delayed replacement, customer frustration

elements.title: Delay in Dispatch Due to Missing Label
elements.scenario_text: An order is packed and ready, but dispatch is delayed by a day because shipping label was not generated.
elements.what_went_wrong: Integration between WMS and courier system failed to auto-generate the label.
elements.who_was_involved: Warehouse Packer, WMS, Courier API, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Label auto-generated at packing stage
Actual: Manual intervention required to print label
elements.impact: Shipping delay, missed SLA, increase in customer support tickets

elements.title: Item Marked as Shipped But Still in Warehouse
elements.scenario_text: Customer sees their order as "Shipped" on the website, but it's actually still lying in the warehouse due to pickup delay.
elements.what_went_wrong: WMS status was updated without actual handover to the courier.
elements.who_was_involved: Warehouse, Courier Partner, OMS, BA, Dev
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Status to update only after package handover
Actual: Shipment status pushed too early
elements.impact: False tracking info, customer confusion, escalations

elements.title: Inventory Count Doesn't Match Physical Stock
elements.scenario_text: WMS reports 150 units of a product in stock, but during physical audit, only 138 units are found.
elements.what_went_wrong: Improper bin movement tracking and missed barcode scanning during internal transfers.
elements.who_was_involved: Warehouse Staff, Inventory Team, WMS, Audit Team
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Digital stock count matches physical stock
Actual: Mismatch caused by system gaps
elements.impact: Reconciliation needed, stock adjustments, order routing errors

elements.title: Order Lost in Transit After Handover
elements.scenario_text: Customer's package was handed over to the courier, but no tracking updates appear, and the customer doesn't receive the item.
elements.what_went_wrong: Courier partner failed to scan the package after pickup; no handoff proof exists.
elements.who_was_involved: Courier Partner, Warehouse, OMS, Customer Support
elements.expected_vs_actual:
🔹 Expected: Tracking to begin immediately after pickup
🔹 Actual: Order marked as picked but vanished in transit
elements.impact: Reshipment needed, refund processing, customer dissatisfaction

elements.title: Loyalty Points Not Credited After Purchase
elements.scenario_text: A loyal customer makes a purchase but doesn't receive the expected reward points in their account.
elements.what_went_wrong: POS failed to communicate the transaction to the loyalty engine.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, POS, Loyalty Engine, CRM, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Points credited automatically after eligible purchase
Actual: No points credited even after 24 hours
elements.impact: Customer complaint, trust issues with loyalty program

elements.title: Expired Coupon Still Accepted at Checkout
elements.scenario_text: A customer uses a coupon that expired yesterday, but the system still applies the discount.
elements.what_went_wrong: Promotion rule cache was not refreshed in the backend system.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, POS/eCom, Promo Engine, BA, Dev
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Expired coupons should be rejected
Actual: System incorrectly honored an expired coupon
elements.impact: Revenue loss, audit exceptions, promo abuse risk

elements.title: Tier Upgrade Not Reflected
elements.scenario_text: Customer reaches gold tier after a purchase milestone but still sees silver tier in their account.
elements.what_went_wrong: CRM rules didn't auto-upgrade the tier due to missing rule trigger.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Loyalty Engine, CRM, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Tier should auto-upgrade once threshold is crossed
Actual: Customer's tier status didn't change
elements.impact: Customer dissatisfaction, perceived unfairness

elements.title: Multiple Promo Codes Applied Together
elements.scenario_text: Customer applies two promo codes at checkout that were not meant to be stackable, but the discount is granted.
elements.what_went_wrong: Promo engine failed to validate exclusion logic.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, eCom/Checkout Engine, Promo Engine, BA, Dev
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Only one code should be accepted
Actual: Both applied, double discount given
elements.impact: Revenue impact, policy violation

elements.title: Points Earned Show Incorrect Value
elements.scenario_text: Customer spends ₹1,000 expecting 100 points (10%), but receives only 50.
elements.what_went_wrong: Wrong rule mapped to the SKU category in loyalty engine.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Loyalty Engine, CRM, BA, QA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Points calculated as per active rate
Actual: Points credited were less than expected
elements.impact: Customer raises complaint, manual correction required

elements.title: Customer Escalation Not Logged in CRM
elements.scenario_text: Customer calls to complain about a missing refund. The support agent resolves it over call, but no record is saved.
elements.what_went_wrong: Agent didn't log the ticket in CRM after the call ended.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Support Agent, CRM, QA Team
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Every issue logged for tracking and analytics
Actual: No audit trail or case created

elements.title: Support Agent Gives Wrong Info About Return Window
elements.scenario_text: Customer is told that the return window is 15 days, but it's actually 30 days. They delay return and then are denied.
elements.what_went_wrong: Agent relied on outdated info from an old document.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Support Agent, CRM, Knowledge Team
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Agent should refer to the updated policy
Actual: Incorrect information shared
elements.impact: Escalation, poor CSAT, social media complaint

elements.title: Refund Status Not Visible to Agent
elements.scenario_text: Customer asks about a refund status, but the agent cannot find the transaction in the system.
elements.what_went_wrong: CRM is not synced with Finance module or OMS.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Support Agent, CRM, Finance System, BA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Refund status should be viewable by support
Actual: Agent had to raise a backend request
elements.impact: Customer inconvenience, delayed response, ticket escalation

elements.title: Multiple Tickets Raised for Same Issue
elements.scenario_text: A customer follows up 3 times on the same issue, and each time a new ticket is created, confusing the resolution team.
elements.what_went_wrong: CRM failed to identify duplicate entries due to missing customer ID linkage.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, CRM, Support Agent, Tech Team
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: System should merge follow-ups under one ticket
Actual: Multiple tickets created
elements.impact: Redundant work, slow resolution, poor customer perception

elements.title: Customer Profile Missing Order History
elements.scenario_text: Customer calls support to ask about a past order, but the agent cannot see any past transactions.
elements.what_went_wrong: Order history sync failed between OMS and CRM.
elements.who_was_involved: Customer, Support Agent, CRM, OMS, BA
elements.expected_vs_actual:
Expected: Full profile including purchase history visible to agent
Actual: CRM showed blank transaction list
elements.impact: Limited support capability, low first-call resolution rate
